title: Survivor Benefits Delivery Improvement Act of 2025
short_title: Survivor Benefits Delivery Improvement Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill makes the Department of Veterans Affairs do a better job helping families of veterans who have died. The VA will collect information about who gets survivor benefits to find out which groups of people are not getting enough help. They will also reach out more to surviving family members to make sure they know about all the benefits they can get. The goal is to make sure all veteran families get the support they deserve after their loved one passes away.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Disability Assistance and Memorial Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Survivor Benefits Delivery Improvement Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Equitable Access to Certain Benefits of Department of Veterans Affairs for Survivors of Veterans
sections.text: This section can be called the Survivor Benefits Data Collection Act of two thousand twenty five. The Department of Veterans Affairs must start collecting information about the backgrounds of people who get survivor benefits. This includes people who get disability payments, pensions, or burial benefits because a veteran died. The Department must work with several advisory committees and veteran service organizations to figure out how to collect this information. The information includes details about race, ethnicity, tribal membership, LGBTQIA plus status, and where people live. The Department must use this information to identify groups of people who are not getting enough help with these benefits. These groups will be called underserved demographics. The Department must review these groups at least every two years and update the list when needed. Each year the Department must include this information in its official report about veteran benefits. No person has to provide this background information, and the Department cannot use whether someone provides it to decide if they get benefits. A covered survivor means a surviving spouse, child, or parent of a veteran. The Department has one hundred eighty days after this law passes to start collecting this information. The Department has one year after it starts collecting information to identify the first underserved groups. The Department must start including this information in annual reports one year after it begins collecting the data. Within one year of identifying underserved groups, the Department must create and send Congress a plan for reaching out to covered survivors in these groups to tell them about available benefits. The Under Secretary for Memorial Affairs must create a plan within one hundred eighty days to reach out to veterans and beneficiaries in underserved groups about burial benefits in national cemeteries. This Under Secretary must also carry out education activities and send Congress yearly reports for five years about these efforts. Within one year of this law passing, the Department must study the resources of the Office of Survivors Assistance and create a plan to make sure this office has what it needs to do its job.

sections.title: Section 3 Improvements to Outreach Services Provided by Secretary of Veterans Affairs to Individuals Who Served in the Uniformed Services and Dependents of Such Individuals
sections.text: This section can be called the Survivor Solid Start Act of two thousand twenty five. The law changes the definition of who can get outreach services from the Department of Veterans Affairs. Now covered individuals include veterans and anyone who served in the uniformed services. When the Department learns that a covered individual has died, it must reach out to their eligible dependents at least once every three months by mail, email, and phone calls. This continues until the dependent files a claim for benefits. If the dependent is a minor child, the Department contacts their legal guardian instead. If the Department does not have contact information, it must make reasonable efforts to find it. Dependents or guardians can choose to get fewer of these contacts or none at all. The Department must tell them about this choice at least once a year. The outreach must give dependents information about the Office of Survivors Assistance, how to get help filing benefit claims from veteran service organizations or attorneys, and how to find these helpers near where they live. When creating materials for this outreach, the Department must work with veteran service organizations and advisory committees for women veterans, minority veterans, and veteran families. The Department must hire at least five but no more than ten full time workers at its call centers to handle this new outreach program.
